TFHE Deep Dive - Part IV: Programmable Bootstrapping

Zama (TFHE Deep Dive series, Part IV) · full view ↗

Overview and context

This is the final installment of Ilaria Chillotti's four-part TFHE Deep Dive series (published 2 June 2022). It covers bootstrapping, the operation introduced by Gentry in 2009 that reduces the noise of a ciphertext so that homomorphic computation can continue indefinitely. In TFHE bootstrapping is special: it does not merely refresh noise, it simultaneously evaluates a function on the encrypted message. This is called programmable bootstrapping (PBS).

The construction reuses the primitives developed in the earlier parts of the series: LWE / GLWE ciphertexts, the external product, and the CMux gate built on GGSW ciphertexts. Bootstrapping is assembled from three building blocks executed in sequence: modulus switching, blind rotation, and sample extraction. The summary below treats each block, then composes them into the full PBS algorithm, and finishes with two applications (gate bootstrapping and homomorphic neural-network inference).

Throughout, an LWE ciphertext of a message $m$ scaled by $\Delta$ is written $c = (a_0, \ldots, a_{n-1}, b) \in \mathbb{Z}_q^{n+1}$, where the decryption phase recovers $b - \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} a_i s_i = \Delta m + e$ for secret key $\vec{s}$, scaling factor $\Delta$ and small noise $e$. The starting observation of bootstrapping is that this phase computation is exactly what must be evaluated homomorphically to refresh a ciphertext.

The core idea: decryption in the exponent

The TFHE approach is to put the computation of (the negation of) the LWE phase

$$b - \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} a_i s_i = \Delta m + e \qquad\text{(Equation 1)}$$

into the exponent of a monomial $X$. If a polynomial $V$ is multiplied by $X^{-(\Delta m + e)}$, then the constant term of the product is the coefficient of $V$ that originally sat at position $\Delta m + e$. By choosing $V$ to be a carefully designed lookup table (LUT), the constant coefficient after rotation becomes the desired (refreshed, and optionally transformed) message.

Doing this homomorphically requires two things: (i) the exponent must be reduced to a usable size, because monomials live modulo $X^N + 1$ and so only $2N$ distinct rotations exist; and (ii) the rotation amount $\sum a_i s_i$ depends on the secret key and must therefore be applied blindly on encrypted data. These two needs are met by modulus switching and blind rotation respectively, after which sample extraction reads out the constant coefficient as a fresh LWE ciphertext.

Building block 1: Modulus switching

Modulus switching converts an LWE ciphertext from a modulus $q$ to a smaller modulus $\omega$. Every component is rescaled and rounded:

$$\tilde{a}_i = \left\lfloor \frac{\omega \cdot a_i}{q} \right\rceil \in \mathbb{Z}_\omega \qquad\text{(Equation 2)}$$

Starting from $c = (a_0, \ldots, a_{n-1}, b) \in \mathrm{LWE}_{\vec{s},\sigma}(\Delta m) \subseteq \mathbb{Z}_q^{n+1}$, the result is $\tilde{c} = (\tilde{a}_0, \ldots, \tilde{a}_{n-1}, \tilde{a}_n = \tilde{b}) \in \mathrm{LWE}_{\vec{s},\sigma}(\tilde{\Delta} m) \subseteq \mathbb{Z}_\omega^{n+1}$, encrypting the same message under the same key $\vec{s}$ but with a new scaling factor

$$\tilde{\Delta} = \frac{\omega \cdot \Delta}{q} = \frac{\omega}{p},$$

where $p$ is the plaintext modulus; the target modulus is chosen as a power of two with $p < \omega < q$. The operation keeps the most significant bits of the original ciphertext, which is what matters because in TFHE the message is encoded in the MSBs. Concretely it preserves the $\log_2(\omega)$ most significant bits. The price is that the relative noise grows (the noise "gets closer to the message"), since rounding to a coarser modulus injects a rounding error.

For bootstrapping, the target modulus is chosen as $\omega = 2N$, where $N$ is the degree of the polynomials in the GLWE ciphertexts used downstream. After the switch the phase becomes

$$\tilde{b} - \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \tilde{a}_i s_i = \tilde{\Delta} m + \tilde{e} \qquad\text{(Equation 3)}$$

with all quantities now living modulo $2N$, exactly the order of the monomial group used by blind rotation.

Building block 2: Sample extraction

Sample extraction takes a GLWE ciphertext, which encrypts a polynomial message, and produces an LWE ciphertext encrypting a single chosen coefficient of that polynomial. Crucially it does not increase the noise: it simply reorganizes the coefficients already present in the GLWE ciphertext.

A GLWE ciphertext has $k$ mask polynomials $A_0, \ldots, A_{k-1}$ and a body polynomial $B$, each of degree $< N$, with coefficients written $a_{i,j}$ and $b_j$. To extract the $h$-th coefficient of the underlying plaintext polynomial as an LWE ciphertext $(a_0, \ldots, a_{kN-1}, b)$, the coefficients are copied according to:

$$\begin{cases} a_{N\cdot i + j} \leftarrow a_{i,\,h-j} & \text{for } 0 \leq i < k,\ 0 \leq j \leq h \\[4pt] a_{N\cdot i + j} \leftarrow -\,a_{i,\,h-j+N} & \text{for } 0 \leq i < k,\ h+1 \leq j < N \\[4pt] b \leftarrow b_h \end{cases} \qquad\text{(Equation 4)}$$

The sign flip in the second case reflects the negacyclic structure of $\mathbb{Z}_q[X]/(X^N+1)$, where wrapping a coefficient index past $N$ negates it. The resulting LWE ciphertext is defined under the extracted secret key, the flattening of the GLWE secret key polynomials into a single vector of length $kN$:

$$\vec{s} = (s_{0,0}, \ldots, s_{0,N-1}, \ldots, s_{k-1,0}, \ldots, s_{k-1,N-1}) \in \mathbb{Z}^{kN}.$$

In bootstrapping, sample extraction reads out the constant coefficient ($h = 0$) of the rotated LUT, which by construction holds the refreshed message.

Building block 3: Rotating a polynomial (clear, then encrypted)

Before the blind version, the article builds intuition with rotation in the clear. Multiplying a polynomial by a monomial $X^{p}$ rotates its coefficients by $p$ positions. Because arithmetic is modulo $X^N + 1$, coefficients pushed past degree $N-1$ wrap around with a sign change (negacyclic rotation). If $p$ is a clear (unencrypted) integer, this rotation can be applied directly to a GLWE ciphertext encrypting the polynomial, simply by multiplying the ciphertext by $X^{p}$; the noise is unchanged because monomial multiplication only permutes and negates coefficients.

The challenge in bootstrapping is that the rotation amount we actually need, $-\tilde{b} + \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \tilde{a}_i s_i$, depends on the secret bits $s_i$ and so is itself secret. The fixed part $X^{-\tilde{b}}$ (with $\tilde{b}$ public) is applied directly; the secret-dependent part $\sum \tilde{a}_i s_i$ must be applied blindly. That is the job of blind rotation.

Building block 4: Blind rotation

Blind rotation rotates an encrypted polynomial by an encrypted amount. It uses the CMux gate iteratively: each CMux either applies a known rotation $X^{\tilde{a}_i}$ or leaves the accumulator unchanged, with the choice driven by an encrypted secret bit. Because the selector bit is hidden inside a GGSW ciphertext, the total number of positions rotated stays encrypted.

The encrypted selector bits are supplied by the bootstrapping key, a set of GGSW ciphertexts each encrypting one bit of the input LWE secret key, under a (generally different) GLWE secret key $\vec{S}'$:

$$\mathrm{BK}_i \in \mathrm{GGSW}^{\beta,\ell}_{\vec{S}',\sigma}(s_i) \subseteq \mathcal{R}_q^{(k+1)\times \ell(k+1)} \qquad\text{(Equation 5)}$$

where $\beta$ is the decomposition base and $\ell$ the number of decomposition levels.

The accumulator is initialized with the LUT polynomial $V$ pre-rotated by the public body term:

$$V_0 = V \cdot X^{-\tilde{b}}.$$

Then for $i = 0, \ldots, n-1$, a CMux selects between the current accumulator and its rotation by $X^{\tilde{a}_i}$, using $\mathrm{BK}_i$ (the encryption of $s_i$) as selector:

$$V_{i+1} = \mathrm{CMux}\bigl(\mathrm{BK}_i,\ V_i \cdot X^{\tilde{a}_i},\ V_i\bigr) \qquad\text{(Equation 6)}$$

so that the extra rotation $X^{\tilde{a}_i}$ is applied if and only if $s_i = 1$. After all $n$ steps the accumulator equals

$$V_n = V \cdot X^{-\tilde{b} + \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \tilde{a}_i s_i} = V \cdot X^{-(\tilde{\Delta} m + \tilde{e})} \qquad\text{(Equation 7)}$$

which is precisely $V$ rotated by the negated phase from Equation 3. The constant coefficient of $V_n$ now sits at the position determined by the message.

The lookup table and the mega-cases

The polynomial $V$ encodes the function being evaluated as a lookup table. Because the rotation amount carries not just the message $\tilde{\Delta} m$ but also the noise $\tilde{e}$, the LUT must be redundant: nearby phase values must map to the same output so that the noise does not change the result. As the article puts it, if the message is $m$, then the values $\Delta m$, $\Delta m + 1$, $\Delta m - 1$, and so on are all sent to $m$.

These redundant blocks of consecutive coefficients all holding the same output value are called mega-cases. Each mega-case spans the window of phase values that decode to one message. After blind rotation by $X^{-(\tilde{\Delta} m + \tilde{e})}$, the constant coefficient lands inside the mega-case for $m$ as long as $|\tilde{e}|$ is smaller than half the mega-case width, so the correct value is selected purely by the polynomial structure rather than by any explicit comparison or branch. This is why bootstrapping both removes noise (the output is a fresh encryption of the clean value) and can apply an arbitrary function (whatever values are written into the mega-cases).

Negacyclicity and the padding bit

Polynomials live in $\mathcal{R} = \mathbb{Z}_q[X]/(X^N+1)$, where the monomial $X$ has order $2N$: rotating by $X^{2N}$ returns to the identity, and crossing $N$ positions flips the sign of a coefficient ($X^N \equiv -1$). This negacyclic behavior constrains which functions can be tabulated directly.

A function is negacyclic when its second half of $N$ coefficients is a copy of the first half with opposite sign, matching the ring relation $X^N \equiv -1$. If the LUT being evaluated is negacyclic, all $p$ possible message values can be used. If it is not, only $p/2$ values are usable, and the standard fix is to reserve one bit of padding (the most significant bit of the message is kept at zero) so that the message never reaches the half of the ring where the sign would flip. The padding bit guarantees the rotation stays within the safe, non-wrapping half.

Putting it together: programmable bootstrapping

The full programmable bootstrapping of an input LWE ciphertext proceeds in three steps:

  1. Modulus switching $q \to 2N$: rescale the input ciphertext so its phase fits in the exponent of a monomial modulo $X^N+1$ (Equation 2, Equation 3).
  2. Blind rotation: rotate the LUT polynomial $V$ (held inside a GLWE accumulator) by the encrypted negated phase, via the iterative CMux loop using the bootstrapping key (Equation 6, Equation 7).
  3. Sample extraction: extract the constant coefficient ($h = 0$) of the rotated accumulator as an LWE ciphertext (Equation 4).

The output is a fresh LWE ciphertext encrypting $f(m)$ with noise that no longer depends on the input noise but only on the bootstrapping parameters. The result is defined under the extracted secret key $\vec{s}$ of dimension $kN$; if one wants to return to the original LWE secret key $\vec{s}'$, an LWE-to-LWE key switching is performed afterward (key switching itself is covered in Part III of the series, not here).

Programmability and hiding the LUT. The LUT polynomial $V$ is generally given un-encrypted (it is public, encoded as a trivial GLWE ciphertext). The choice of $V$ is what makes bootstrapping "programmable": any function representable as a (negacyclic) table can be evaluated for free during the noise refresh. If the evaluated function must itself be secret, $V$ can be supplied as a non-trivial GLWE encryption, so the LUT is hidden from the party performing the bootstrapping, with almost no impact on performance.

Application 1: Gate bootstrapping

Gate bootstrapping evaluates a Boolean logic gate while simultaneously refreshing the noise, producing an output formatted to feed directly into the next gate. A bit is encoded in a special way: 0 is encoded as $-q/8$ and 1 as $q/8$. To evaluate a gate (for example AND/NAND), the procedure is:

  1. Form a linear combination of the two input ciphertexts (plus, where needed, a constant), so that the combined phase falls into the region encoding the correct gate output.
  2. Apply a bootstrapping, followed by a key switching to bring the result back to the original key.

The bootstrapping evaluates the LUT encoded in the polynomial

$$V = \sum_{j=0}^{N-1} \frac{q}{8} X^j \qquad\text{(Equation 8)}$$

which is a rescaled sign function: it outputs $\tfrac{q}{8}$ for all messages with positive sign and $-\tfrac{q}{8}$ for all messages with negative sign. Because this sign function is negacyclic, no bit of padding is needed, so the full message space is available.

Application 2: Homomorphic neural-network inference

Programmable bootstrapping is well suited to evaluating artificial neural networks homomorphically. Integer inputs are encrypted, and each neuron's computation is split into the two parts PBS handles naturally:

  • the linear combination (weighted sum of inputs plus bias) is evaluated with homomorphic additions and scalar multiplications on LWE ciphertexts;
  • the activation function is evaluated by a programmable bootstrapping whose LUT encodes that activation, which at the same time refreshes the noise so the next layer can be computed.

Because each activation is realized as a bootstrap, the network can be made arbitrarily deep: every nonlinearity doubles as a noise reset. This makes TFHE's programmable bootstrapping a natural primitive for encrypted deep-learning inference.

Algorithms

The complete algorithms referenced above, written as self-contained pseudocode.

Modulus Switching (LWE, $q \to \omega$).

Input:  c = (a_0, ..., a_{n-1}, b) in LWE_s(Delta * m) mod q
        target modulus omega   (for bootstrapping, omega = 2N)
Output: c~ in LWE_s(Delta~ * m) mod omega, with Delta~ = omega*Delta/q

1. for i = 0 to n-1:
       a~_i = round( omega * a_i / q )   mod omega
2. b~   = round( omega * b   / q )       mod omega
3. return (a~_0, ..., a~_{n-1}, b~)

Sample Extraction (GLWE -> LWE, coefficient h).

Input:  GLWE ct with mask polys A_0..A_{k-1} (coeffs a_{i,j}) and body B (coeffs b_j)
        coefficient index h    (h = 0 for the constant term in bootstrapping)
Output: LWE ct (a_0, ..., a_{kN-1}, b) under extracted key s of dim kN

1. for i = 0 to k-1:
       for j = 0 to h:
           a_{N*i + j} =  a_{i, h-j}
       for j = h+1 to N-1:
           a_{N*i + j} = -a_{i, h-j+N}     # negacyclic sign flip on wrap
2. b = b_h
3. return (a_0, ..., a_{kN-1}, b)

Blind Rotation (CMux loop).

Input:  modulus-switched ct (a~_0, ..., a~_{n-1}, b~) mod 2N
        LUT polynomial V (inside a GLWE accumulator)
        bootstrapping key BK_i = GGSW_{S'}(s_i), i = 0..n-1
Output: GLWE accumulator V_n = V * X^{ -b~ + sum_i a~_i s_i }

1. ACC = V * X^{-b~}                         # V_0, public rotation by body
2. for i = 0 to n-1:
       ACC = CMux( BK_i, ACC * X^{a~_i}, ACC )  # apply X^{a~_i} iff s_i = 1
3. return ACC                                # = V * X^{-(Delta~ m + e~)}

Programmable Bootstrapping (full).

Input:  LWE ct c in LWE_s(Delta * m) mod q
        LUT polynomial V encoding the function f (negacyclic, or use 1 padding bit)
        bootstrapping key BK = { GGSW_{S'}(s_i) }
        (optional) key-switching key to return to original key
Output: LWE ct of f(m), noise independent of input noise

1. c~  = ModulusSwitch(c, omega = 2N)        # phase fits exponent mod X^N+1
2. ACC = BlindRotate(c~, V, BK)              # rotate LUT by encrypted phase
3. c_out = SampleExtract(ACC, h = 0)         # read constant coefficient -> LWE
4. (optional) c_out = KeySwitch(c_out)       # back to original LWE key (Part III)
5. return c_out

Gate Bootstrapping (Boolean gate, e.g. AND/NAND).

Encoding: bit 0 -> -q/8 ,  bit 1 -> q/8
LUT:      V = sum_{j=0}^{N-1} (q/8) X^j      # rescaled sign function (negacyclic)

Input:  LWE ciphertexts c1, c2 of input bits
Output: LWE ciphertext of gate(c1, c2), refreshed

1. c_lin = (linear combination of c1, c2) [+ constant]   # land in correct region
2. c_bs  = Bootstrap(c_lin, V)               # evaluates rescaled sign, refreshes noise
3. c_out = KeySwitch(c_bs)                    # return to original key
4. return c_out
